draft.introduction: In Metaphysics XII.7, Aristotle presents his theology of the Unmoved Mover—the eternal, unchanging first cause that moves all things by being the object of their desire. This divine being is pure actuality, engaged in eternal contemplation.

translation.text: There is something which moves without being moved, being eternal, substance, and actuality. The object of desire and the object of thought move in this way: they move without being moved. The first mover, then, exists of necessity; and in so far as it exists by necessity, its mode of being is good, and it is in this sense a first principle. It is upon such a principle that the heavens and nature depend.
translation.original_text: ἔστι τι ὃ οὐ κινούμενον κινεῖ, ἀΐδιον καὶ οὐσία καὶ ἐνέργεια οὖσα

translator_notes.note: The Unmoved Mover moves “as an object of love” (κινεῖ δὲ ὡς ἐρώμενον): it attracts all things toward itself without acting upon them. This concept profoundly influenced the medieval theological tradition, where it was identified with the Christian God.
